feedback.text: One of the most exploitative industries operating predominantly in the digital space is gaming. It showcases nearly every known dark pattern: misleading advertising, post-purchase redefinitions of terms like "purchase" and "ownership" in EULAs or Terms of Service, the revocation of access to paid-for products, and the intentional design of addictive, time-consuming mechanics to coax players into spending ever more, often through gambling-like systems or monetized progression. As a gamer with over 30 years of experience, the two most pressing issues are: 1. Deceptive and misleading marketing, both in text and visuals, which gives consumers an impression of the product that often does not reflect the final experience. At best, this marketing may be technically correct, but it is crafted to mislead, distort expectations, and justify predatory sales tactics. That should not be acceptable. 2. Redefining fundamental consumer terms like purchase and own, allowing companies to revoke access to digital products at any time, for any or no reason. These rights are typically buried deep in post-purchase EULAs, which consumers often cannot review until after the transaction. There are many examples of companies changing or disabling a product after sale to push users toward sequels, microtransactions, or other services, or simply to cut costs if the product did not meet their arbitrary expectations. To illustrate: Imagine buying a car that must stay connected to a server to function. If that server is shut down, the car stops working. Imagine an update pushed to your vehicles software that reduces its functionality to pressure you into buying the next model. Imagine a situation where you are forced to register your car in such a way that it becomes practically impossible to resell. These scenarios would be unthinkable in the traditional, physical world; yet they are the norm in digital gaming. This is a clear abuse of regulatory loopholes and a violation of core EU consumer protection principles. As harmful as gambling establishments, if not more so, due to frequent purposeful targeting of children and compulsive users. The evidence for these issues is abundant, through independent reports, academic studies, and even casual searches online. It is a systemic problem. Any regulation should include, at minimum: Prohibiting the redefinition of terms like purchase and ownership. Mandating a clear distinction between genuine purchases (with rights of retention and access) and rental or license-based models (with disclosed time-limited access). Requiring an enforceable end-of-life plan for digital products, that ensures users retain meaningful access to purchases even after official support ends; and a clear, time-based guarantee for licensed content. Protecting product integrity by ensuring that purchased products remain as advertised and originally sold, unless explicitly and voluntarily agreed to by the user. Requiring that all essential contract terms (e.g., access rights, revocation conditions, server dependency) are presented clearly and prominently before purchase, such that a layman would understand it. Recognizing a consumers right to resell or transfer their digital purchases, in line with the EUs broader stance on the exhaustion of distribution rights. Prohibit unnecessary server dependencies for single-player or non-networked functionality, and require that products remain usable after official support ends. Where online components are essential, enable continued accessthrough private server support, peer-to-peer connectivity, or direct IP connectionsas far as technically possible. This should include tools, documentation, and stripped-down binaries. Games must be designed with this in mind from the outset. This is not an exhaustive analysis, but a personal comment based on long experience, meant to highlight urgent issues and raise awareness.
